narration: The archive has eight aisles. Every map on the wall says so. Every page of the ledger says so. So I need someone to explain the aisle I walked into at 3 AM — the one after eight. The boxes there don't have file numbers. They have a name. Mine. Hundreds of boxes. Years on the labels I haven't lived yet... and years from before I was born. I opened the oldest one. Inside: photographs. A birthday party in a house I've never been to. A boy who looks exactly like I did. And a staff card for this archive — my face, my name... issued 1987. I put it back. I swear I put it back. Behind me, at the end of the aisle... a box slid off the shelf on its own.

shots.prompt: Ihsan (charcoal work shirt, staff badge, flashlight in hand) walks past the painted aisle numbers on the concrete floor, his beam sweeping across them one by one — seven, then eight — then he stops dead: beyond the last shelf, another aisle mouth opens into darkness. Ends on: his flashlight beam trembling on the extra aisle.

Ihsan (charcoal work shirt) moves slowly into the impossible aisle: every box on every shelf carries the same handwritten name label instead of a file number; he pulls one box out, reads the label, shoves it back, pulls another — the same name again; his breathing quickens as the aisle stretches deeper. Ends on: rows of identical name labels receding into the dark.

Back at the desk Ihsan (charcoal work shirt) opens the oldest, most sun-yellowed box under the green lamp: faded photographs spill across the ledger; he lifts one close to the light — a small birthday party in an unfamiliar living room, a little boy at its center. Ends on: his thumb hovering over the boy's face.

From the same box Ihsan (charcoal work shirt) lifts a yellowed laminated staff card into the green lamplight and goes completely still: the small photo on the vintage 1980s card is HIS OWN face; he wipes the dust from it with his thumb, then slowly raises the card beside his head. Ends on: his real face and the card's identical face side by side in the lamplight.

Far down the impossible aisle a single box slides off its shelf by itself and drops to the concrete with a heavy echoing thud; dust erupts upward through the beam of a caged bulb; the fallen box rocks once and lies still. Ends on: the box lying in the pool of light, its lid slightly ajar.

Ihsan (charcoal work shirt) stands frozen at the mouth of the aisle staring at the distant fallen box, flashlight shaking slightly in his hand — above him the caged bulbs begin going dark one by one, the darkness walking toward him down the aisle. Ends on: only his silhouette left against the last burning bulb.
